concepts.title: Physical state
concepts.conceptBody:
Physical Properties

  1. Physical state: They are all silvery white metals. They have greyish white lustre when freshly cut, but tarnish soon after their exposure in air due to surface oxidation. They are soft in nature but harder than alkali metals because metallic bonding is stronger than IA elements due to possession of 2 valency electrons. However, hardness decreases with increase in atomic number. These elements are malleable and ductile but less than alkali metals.

concepts.title: Atomic size and Density
concepts.conceptBody:
Atomic and ionic radii: 

The size of the atom increases gradually from $\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ra}$, on account of the presence of an extra energy shell at each step. The atoms are large but smaller than corresponding IA elements since the extra charge on the nucleus attracts the electron cloud inwards. Their ions are also large and size of the ion increases from $\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}$ to $\text{R}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}$.

Atomic volume also increases as the atomic number increases.

(c) Density: These metals are denser than alkali metals in the same period because these can be packed more tightly due to their greater nuclear charge and smaller size. The density decreases slightly upto calcium and then increases considerably upto radium. Irregular trend is due to the difference in the crystal structure of these elements.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{Be} & \text{Mg} & \text{Ca} & \text{Sr} & \text{Ba} & \text{Ra} \\ \text{ Density }\left( \text{gm}{{\text{l}}^{-1}} \right) & 1.84 & 1.74 & 1.55 & 2.63 & 3.62 & 5.5 \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Melting and Boiling Points
concepts.conceptBody:
Melting and boiling points: 

The melting and boiling points of these elements are higher than corresponding alkali metals. This is due to the presence of two electrons in the valency shell and thus, strongly bonded in the solid state. However, melting and boiling points do not show any regular trend because atoms adopt different crystal structures.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{Be} & \text{Mg} & \text{Ca} & \text{Sr} & \text{Ba} & \text{Ra} \\ \text{ m}\text{. pt}\text{. }\left( ^{{}^\circ }\text{C} \right) & 1277 & 650 & 838 & 763 & 714 & 700 \\ \text{ b}\text{. pt}\text{. }\left( ^{{}^\circ }\text{C} \right) & 2770 & 1107 & 1440 & 1380 & 1640 & 1140 \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Ionisation energies and Electropositive character
concepts.conceptBody:
Ionisation energies and electropositive character: 

The first and second ionisation energies of these metals decrease from$\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ba}$. The second ionisation energy in each case is higher than the first, nearly double the first ionisation energy.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{Be} & \text{Mg} & \text{Ca} & \text{Sr} & \text{Ba} & \text{Ra} \\ \text{ First I}\text{.E}\text{. (eV) } & 9.32 & 7.64 & 6.11 & 5.70 & 5.2 & 5.3 \\ \text{ Second I}\text{.E}\text{. (eV) } & 18.21 & 15.03 & 11.87 & 11.0 & 10.0 & 10.1 \\ \end{matrix}$

The ionisation energy of last member, radium, is slightly higher than that of barium and it is barium and it is difficult to explain this anomalous behaviour.

Although, the ionisation energies of these elements are higher than those of alkali metals, yet these are sufficiently low to make these atoms to lose two electrons of their valency shell to form ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$ ions and achieve the inert gas configuration. These metals are thus, strongly electropositive in nature but less than corresponding alkali metals. The electropositive character increases from $\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ba}$. Metallic character and reactivity are directly linked with the tendency to lose electron or electrons, i.e., with electropositive nature. Thus, these characters increase gradually from $\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ba}$.

concepts.title: Oxidation states
concepts.conceptBody:
(f) Oxidation states: All show a stable oxidation state +2 in their compounds. The second ionisation energy is nearly double the first ionisation energy for all elements. This should cause these elements of exhibit a stable +1 oxidation state and form compounds like $\text{BaCl}$, $\text{SrBr},\text{CaI}$, etc., instead of $\text{BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{SrB}{{\text{r}}_{2}},\text{Ca}{{\text{I}}_{2}}$, etc. However, the lattice energy increases as the charge on the ion increases. The increase in the lattice energy on account of the second electron from $\text{n}{{\text{s}}^{2}}$ is much more than the energy required (second ionisation energy) to remove it. Hence, the stability of +2 oxidation state is due to high lattice energy. The second factor responsible for +2 oxidation state is the hydration energy which is high for ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$ ions. On account of the availability of energy, the process does not stop of ${{\text{M}}^{+}}$state but reach to ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$ state readily.
Since the bivalent ions, ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$, have an inert gas configuration, it is very difficult to remove the third electron and hence oxidation state higher than +2 is not possible.
Amongst alkaline earth metals, beryllium has the highest ionisation energy, i.e., least electropositive in nature. Thus, beryllium has the minimum tendency to form $\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}$ ion and hence a number of compounds of beryllium are covalent in nature.

concepts.title: Hydrtion of ions and Hydration energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydration of ions and hydration energy: 

The ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$ ions of alkaline earth metals are extensively hydrated to form hydrated ions, ${{\left[ \text{M}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{\text{x}}} \right]}^{2+}}$ and during hydration a huge amount of energy, called hydration energy, is released.

${{\text{M}}^{2+}}+\text{x}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\left[ \text{M}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{\text{x}}} \right]}^{2+}}+\text{ Energy }$

The degree of hydration and the amount of hydration energy decreases as the size of the ion increases from $\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}$ to $\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}$.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}} & \text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}} & \text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}} & \text{S}{{\text{r}}^{2+}} & \text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}} \\ \text{ Heats of hydration } & 570 & 460 & 395 & 355 & 305 \\ (\text{kcal}\,\,\text{mol}\,\,{{\text{m}}^{-1}})\text{ } & {} & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ \end{matrix}$

The hydration energies of alkaline earth metal ions are higher than those of alkali metal ions and thus the compounds of alkaline earth metals are more extensively hydrated than alkali metals. Magnesium chloride and calcium chloride exist as $\text{MgC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot 6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ and $\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot 6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$, respectively, while sodium chloride and potassium chloride exist as $\text{NaCl}$ and $\text{KCl}$.

The ionic mobilities or ionic conductance of these ions increase from ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{\text{x}}} \right]}^{2+}}$ to ${{\left[ \text{Ba}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{x}} \right]}^{2+}}$ because ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{x}} \right]}^{2+}}$ becomes heavy due to high degree of hydration.

concepts.title: Electronegativity,conductivity
concepts.conceptBody:
Electronegativity: 

The tendency to attract electrons is low. The electronegativity values are thus small and decrease from $\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ra}$.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{Be} & \text{Mg} & \text{Ca} & \text{Sr} & \text{Ba} & \text{Ra} \\ \text{ Electronegativity } & 1.47 & 1.23 & 1.04 & 1.0 & 0.97 & 0.97 \\ \end{matrix}$

  1. Conductivity: On account of the presence of two loosely bond valency electrons per atom which can move freely through the crystal lattice, the alkaline earth metals are good conductors of heat and electricity.

concepts.title: Flame Colouration
concepts.conceptBody:
(j) Flame colouration: In the case of $\text{Ca},\text{Sr},\text{Ba}$ and $\text{Ra}$, the electrons can be excited by the supply of energy to higher energy levels. When the excited electrons return to the original level, the energy is released in the form of light. In beryllium and magnesium, the electrons are tightly held and hence excitation is rather difficult, thus do not show flame colouration. $\text{Ca},\text{Sr},\text{Ba}$ and $\text{Ra}$ impart a characteristic colour to the flame.
$\text{Ca-Brick red }\quad \text{Sr-crimson }\quad \text{Ba-green }\quad \text{Ra}-\text{ crimson }$

concepts.title: Reducing Nature
concepts.conceptBody:
Reducing nature: 

The alkaline earth metals have the tendency to lose electron and change into bivalent cation:

$\text{M}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{M}}^{2+}}+2\text{e}$

Hence, they act as strong reducing agents. The reducing nature increase as the atomic number increases.

Strength of a reducing agent is linked with the value of oxidation potential. The values of the oxidation potentials increase from $\text{Be}$ to $\text{Ba}$, hence the strength as reducing agent increases in the same order.

$\begin{matrix} {} & \text{Be} & \text{Mg} & \text{Ca} & \text{Sr} & \text{Ba} \\ \text{ Oxid}\text{. potential (volt) } & 1.85 & 2.37 & 2.87 & 2.89 & 2.90 \\ \end{matrix}$

The oxidation potentials are lower than those of the alkali metals, hence, the alkaline earth metals are weaker reducing agents than alkali metals. The reason for the lower values of oxidation potentials is due to high heats of atomisation (sublimation) and ionisation energies.

concepts.title: Colour and Magnetic properties
concepts.conceptBody:
(1) Colour and magnetic property: Since, the divalent ions have noble gas configuration with no unpaired electrons, their compounds are diamagnetic and colourless unless the anion is coloured. The metals are also diamagnetic in nature as all the orbitals are fully filled with spin paired electrons, e.g.

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}^{2+}}\text{ }=2,8$

$=1{{s}^{2}},2{{s}^{2}}2{{p}^{6}}$

concepts.title: Reactivity of group 2
concepts.conceptBody:
Chemical Properties of Alkaline Earth Elements

Table summarizes reactions of Group 2(IIA). From this, it is evident that as we go down the group, reactivity increases.

Reactions of Group 2 (IIA)

concepts.title: Properties of Group 2
concepts.conceptBody:
Some points to note:

1. Be is amphoteric, as it reacts with acid as well as with base

$\text{Be}+2\text{NaOH}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{Sodium beryllate}}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\left[ \text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]}}\,+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

$\text{Mg},\text{Ca},\text{Sr}$ and $\text{Ba}$ do not react with $\text{NaOH}$ and thus are purely basic.

2. Except beryllium, all combine with hydrogen directly to form hydrides of the type $\text{M}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ when heated with hydrogen

$\text{M}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

$\text{Be}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ and $\text{Mg}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ are covalent in nature while other hydrides are ionic in nature. $\text{Be}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ is formed by reacting beryllium chloride with lithium aluminium hydride

$2\text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Be}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+\text{LiCl}+\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$

It is polymeric. ${{\left( \text{Be}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{\text{n}}}$ process hydrogen bridges. Three centre bonds are present in which a banana shaped molecular orbital covers three atoms $\text{Be}----\text{H}$------ $\text{Be}$ and contains two electrons. Hydrogen a tons lie in the plane perpendicular to the plane of molecule containing beryllium atoms.

3. Alkaline earth metals dissolve in liquid ammonia to form coloured solutions.

$\text{M}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{M}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

$2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{NH}_{2}^{-}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$

${{\text{M}}^{2+}}+2\text{NH}_{2}^{-}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}$

When the solution is evaporated, hex ammoniate, $\text{M}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}}$ is formed. These slowly decompose to give amides

$\text{M}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{6}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)}_{2}}+4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}(\uparrow )+{{\text{H}}_{2}}(\uparrow )$

4. Carbides: The carbides are ionic in nature and have $\text{NaCl}$ type of structure with ${{\text{M}}^{2+}}$ replacing $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$and $\text{C}\equiv {{\text{C}}^{2-}}$ replacing $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$. Beryllium forms methanide, $\text{B}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}$, with carbon and acetylide, $\text{Be}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$, with acetylene. Magnesium on heating with carbon forms $\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$ also, which is an allylide since with water it liberates allylene

$\underset{\text{carbide}}{\mathop{\text{Ca}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}}\,+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\underset{\text{acetylene}}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}}}\,$

\[\text{B}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{C}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\underset{\text{methane}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}}}\,\]

$\underset{\text{allylide}}{\mathop{\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}}}\,+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{Mg}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}+\underset{\text{allylene}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{CH}}}\,$

5. Complex formation: Generally, the alkaline earth metals do not form complexes. However, the smaller ions have some tendency to form complexes. Beryllium forms stable complexes such as ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{3}} \right]}^{-}},{{\left[ \text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$ and ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}$

The most important complex formed by magnesium is chlorophyll in which magnesium is bonded to the four heterocyclic nitrogen atoms. The rest of the elements from calcium to barium form complexes only with strong complexation agents such as acetylacetone, ethylene diamine teraacetic acid (EDTA), etc.

concepts.title: Anamolous Behaviour of Beryllium
concepts.conceptBody:
Anomalous Behaviour of Beryllium

Be differs form the rest of the group for three reasons.

1. It is extremely small and Fajan's rule state that small highly charged ions tend to form covalent compounds.

2. Be has a comparatively high electronegativity. Thus when beryllium reacts with another atom, the difference in electronegativity is seldom large, which again favours the formation of covalent compounds. Even $\text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ (electronegativity difference 2.5) and $\text{BeO}$ (electronegativity difference 2.0) show evidence of covalent character.

3. $\text{Be}$ is in the second row of the periodic table and the outer shell can hold a maximum of eight electrons. (The orbitals available for bonding are one $2~\text{s}$ and three $2\text{p}$ orbitals. Thus $\text{Be}$ can form a maximum of four conventional electron pair bonds and in many compounds the maximum coordination number of $\text{Be}$ is 4 . The later elements can have more than eight outer electrons and may attain a coordination number of 6 using one ' $s$ ', three ' $\text{p}$ ' and two ' $d$ ' orbitals for bonding. Exceptions occur if multi-centre bonding occurs, as for example in basic beryllium acetate, when higher coordination numbers are obtained.

Thus, we should expect $\text{Be}$ to form mainly covalent compounds and commonly have a coordination number 4 . Anhydrous compounds of $\text{Be}$ are predominantly two-covalent and $\text{Be}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$ molecules should be linear.

In fact linear molecules exist only in the gas phase, as this electronic arrangement has not filed the outer shell of electrons. In the solid state four fold coordination is always achieved. There are several ways by which this can be achieved.

1. Two ligands that have a lone pair of electrons may form coordinate bonds using the two unfilled orbitals in the valence shell of $\text{Be}$. Thus two ${{\text{F}}^{-}}$ions might coordinate of $\text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$, forming ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$. Similarly diethyl ether can coordinate to $\text{Be}(+\text{II})$ in $\text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$, forming $\left[ \text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}{{(\text{OEt})}_{2}} \right]$.

2. The $\text{Be}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$ molecules may polymerise to form chains, containing bridging halogen groups, for example ${{\left( \text{Be}{{\text{F}}_{2}} \right)}_{\text{n}}},{{\left( \text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)}_{\text{n}}}$. Each halogen forms one normal covalent bond and uses a lone pair to form a coordinate bond.

3. ${{\left( \text{BeM}{{\text{e}}_{2}} \right)}_{\text{n}}}$ has essentially the same structure as ${{\left( \text{BeC}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)}_{\text{n}}}$, but the bonding in the methyl compound is best regarded as three-centre two electron bonds covering one $\text{Me}$ and two $\text{Be}$ atoms.

4. A covalent lattice may be formed with a zinc blende or wurtzite structure (coordination number 4), for example by $\text{BeO}$ and $\text{BeS}$.

In water beryllium salts are extensively hydrolysed to give a series of hydroxo complexes of unknown structure. They may be polymeric and of the type:

If alkali is added to these solutions, the polymers break down to give the simple mononuclear berrylate ion ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$, which is tetrahedral. Many beryllium salts contain the hydrated ion ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$, which is tetrahedral. Many beryllium salts contain the hydrated ion ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}$, rather than $\text{B}{{\text{e}}^{2+}}$ and the hydrated ion too is a tetrahedral complex ion. Note

that the coordination number is 4 . Forming a hydrated complex increases the effective size of the beryllium ion, thus spreading the charge over a larger area. Stable ionic salts such as $\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}},\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$ and $\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$, are known.

Beryllium salts are acidic when dissolved in pure water because the hydrated ion hydrolyses producing ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$. This happens because the $\text{Be}-\text{O}$ bond is very strong and so in the hydrated ion this weakens the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bonds and hence there is a tendency to lose protons. The initial reaction is

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{4}} \right]}^{2+}}\rightleftharpoons {{\left[ \text{Be}{{\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)}_{3}}(\text{OH}) \right]}^{+}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

but this may be followed by further polymerization, involving hydroxobridged structures. In alkaline solutions ${{\left[ \text{Be}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]}^{2-}}$ is formed. The outer group II salts do not interact so strongly with water and do not hydrolyse appreciably.

Beryllium salts rarely have more than four molecules of water of crystallization associated with the metal ion, because there are only four orbitals available in the second shell of electrons, whereas magnesium can have a coordination number of 6 by using some $3~\text{d}$ orbitals as well as $3~\text{s}$ and $3\text{p}$ orbitals.
